utterances: I think people in the United States want to make a statement about genocide in the Sudan.

And this answers, this economic divestment campaign, answers the question: what can an individual in his or her organizations, in his or her own employment situation, what can individuals do who are concerned about the situation, about protecting human rights.
And this gives them something to do.
Glenn, you are, of course, at Brown University.
I think, what can the individual do, and there was that Olympic Gold winner who gave all of his prize money to the cause of the Sudan.
What is the mood on campus, and how visible is this issue on campus?
Well, not only am I at Brown, I served on the committee advising our president, Ruth Simmons, about this very matter.
And, these symbolic gestures are important, and as Michael says, they do give people something to do and a way of making a statement.
But--and the but that I have to add here is--in my view, sanctions don't work.

We really need diplomacy and we really need the international community to come together in a serious way through the United Nations, through the organization of African states and otherwise, to isolate the Sudanese government and to condemn what's being done there.
While support the sanctions movement?
I mean, how could one not do so?
What's happening on the ground in Darfur is awful, absolutely awful.
I support the movement.

I also recognize that it's largely a set of symbolic gestures, and it has more to do with the psychology of us, giving us something to do and a way to feel that we're fighting this, then it will have to do with the things that are happening on the ground.
To change the things on the ground, we need the governments of the world to come together in a determined way to isolate the Sudanese regime.
And if necessary, to use stronger measures to deal with what's happening there.
Those are the only ways that the facts on the ground are going to change.
I don't think that sanctions by themselves will do it.
Agreed.
That's very…that's very true.
But one of the things here is that, by having various organizations divest, or call for divestment, it brings the issue to people who normally won't be focusing on this.
There are people making presentations in churches, in community centers, and, you know, any kind of local civic gathering.
And, in some cases, people are hearing about Sudan for the first time.
So this is a good thing, and yes, it will give people something to do.
But maybe they can also put more pressure on the politicians and others who have the real authority to get some action here.
Absolutely.
E.R., like Glenn, you're in an academic environment.
Very quickly, last topic.
A college student at the University of the Cumberlands, which is a Baptist University in Kentucky, declared on the hot website MySpace.com that he was gay.

School officials found out, confronted him, expelled him--their student handbook says any student who engages in or promotes sexual behavior not consistent with Christian principles, including sex outside marriage and homosexuality, may be suspended or asked to withdraw.
But the handbook didn't put homosexual in on the year when Johnson enrolled in the school.
Now…
Well, it's sex outside of marriage.
Yes.
So, E.R., what do you think of this?
Does this kid have grounds, I mean, to basically challenge the school?
Well, he will challenge the school, this being the United States of America.
I'm sure litigation is on the horizon.

But, he chose a conservative, Baptist school that has these kinds of rules--whether they were written down and spelled out as clearly when he entered a couple of years ago as they are now--is the question.
But, I think sometimes, one has to be a little bit more responsible themselves.
If you put yourself in an environment where you know what the rules are as you go in.
Well, one cannot really say this student, who is a sophomore, knew the rules.

Because, when you look at the rules, the so-called rules, in the 2003-04 student handbook, all it said was that students must conduct themselves, on and off campus, in a manner that is consistent with the objectives of the college and with its standards of conduct.
So, I say to this student, Yeah, sue them!
And good luck at it!
But I want to say to the students who are now present, and who may go there, why?
This is not serving the purpose of higher education.
It may be a faith-based institution, but for me, higher education is broadening one's horizons; removing narrow constrictions of the mind; extirpating prejudice.
prejudice and ignorance.
And somebody ought to know better, and the Christians out to speak up.
Glenn, we've got 30 seconds.
Yeah, I just want to say I don't agree with Michael here, in this.
Look, let's be tolerant, not only of people who choose a gay or homosexual way of life, but also of people who--for their deeply-held convictions--think that that's wrong.
We're a big enough country to accommodate both.
It's not a problem in my mind that there are some people who look at homosexual behavior and say, No, not for me, not in my church, et cetera.
We can all live together here.
So I think that there needs to be a little bit more tolerance for the people who feel, very strongly, that homosexuality is wrong.
They're not tolerant.
They expelled the student!
(Unintelligible), all of these deeply conservative Christians who think that this stuff is wrong are not bigots for that.
They have a view, a way of life.
It has a long history and tradition in--not only our country, but in many other countries around the world--and we're big enough to accommodate both.
All right, we're going to…we're completely…
They can keep that bigotry and their views outside of our bedrooms.
…out of time.
All right.
That was Glenn Loury, professor of economics at Brown University, from WRNI in Providence.
Michael Meyers, executive director of the New York Civil Rights Coalition; and E.R.
Shipp, professor of journalism at Hofstra University.
Thanks all.
(Together) Thanks.

Time now for your comments.
We received several e-mails on our roundtable discussion.

Our panelists' comments about an online poll which found that the Reverend Jesse Jackson remains the most important black leader to African Americans didn't sit well with Cedric Campbell of Houston, Texas.
He writes,

(reading) It's the height of both irony and hypocrisy that members of a roundtable will publicly say that a list of black leaders is irresponsible while they obviously are on a list of black spokespersons worthy of appearing regularly on your roundtable.
Doug McMullin from Brooklyn, New York, took issue with panelist Michael Myers' remarks on torture and U.N.
calls to close the facility at Guantanamo Bay.
(Soundbite of Michael Myers) Well, what do you want us to do with people who are regarded as dangerous?
And particularly people who are, if released, are likely to not only be dangerous, but help provoke an attack on United States sovereignty.
McMullin writes
(Reading) that views are contemptible and morally blind as this are aired on NPR is fine.
Free debate should mean no thought or idea is prescribed.

However, when opinions this corrupt come from an NPR commentator himself in a panel format, and when the views are not challenged in any way by the host or other panelists, when instead the topic is blithely changed to African American hairstyles on the college campus, I am disgusted and saddened.
From Jacksonville, Florida, Catherine McGoldrich writes about our discussion of the sheriff in Pinellas, Florida who has decided to suspend adulterous deputies.
She writes,
(Reading) I agree with your guest that what happens in private should be just that, private and protected.
However, law enforcement personnel take an oath to uphold the laws of the jurisdictions where they serve.
I am sure the sheriff's deputies in the story took an oath to uphold Florida laws.
That for them includes all of the laws of the state, frivolous or not.
And we received this voicemail from Marjorie Lewis from Ridgewood, New Jersey:
(Voicemail of Marjorie Lewis) Just a comment on the Brazilian Samba Feast about Lent.
Carnival isn't during Lent.
Carnival usually precedes Lent, it's the wild time leading up to Lent.
But other than that, I enjoyed the piece very much.
Thanks for the correction, Marjorie.
And from Atlanta, Georgia, Barbara Jones writes:
(Reading) This is undoubtedly the best hour on radio or television where news by and about black people is concerned.
Your West Coast host, Farai Chideya is absolutely the best I've ever heard.
But not everyone shares that sentiment.
Margaret Sesay of Brooklyn, New York says she was outraged by our special roundtable on street lit.
She writes:
